prompt: I'm using tokio::sync::mpsc to send messages from multiple async tasks to a single receiver. I cloned the Sender and try to use it in a spawn, but I get a compile error about the Sender not being Sync.
error_message:
error[E0277]: `Sender<String>` cannot be shared between threads safely
 --> src/main.rs:12:9
  |
12|     tokio::spawn(async move {
  |     ^^^^^^^^^^^^ `Sender<String>` cannot be shared between threads safely
  |
  = help: the trait `Sync` is not implemented for `Sender<String>`
  = note: required because it appears within the type `Arc<Sender<String>>`
  = note: required because `Arc<Sender<String>>` implements `Deref` which requires its inner type to be `Sync`
explanation: tokio::sync::mpsc::Sender<T> is Clone but not Sync — it uses interior mutability that is safe to clone but not to share by reference across threads. Arc<Sender> tries to share a &Sender across threads, which requires Sync. The correct pattern is to just clone the Sender: each clone is an independent handle to the same channel, tracking its own reference count. The channel closes (rx.recv() returns None) only after ALL Sender clones have been dropped, so drop the original tx after spawning tasks if you want the receiver loop to terminate.

prompt: I'm using tokio::sync::oneshot to get a result back from a spawned task. I accidentally try to send on the Sender twice and need to understand why only one message is possible.
error_message:
error[E0382]: use of moved value: `tx`
 --> src/main.rs:9:9
  |
8 |         tx.send(42).unwrap();
  |            ---- `tx` moved here (send consumes the Sender)
9 |         tx.send(43).unwrap();
  |         ^^ value used here after move
explanation: tokio::sync::oneshot is designed for a single message: send() takes ownership of the Sender (consuming it), making a second send impossible at compile time. This is an intentional design — it encodes the one-shot contract in the type system. The Receiver's await returns Err if the Sender was dropped without sending (the task panicked, or send() was skipped). Use oneshot when a task needs to return exactly one result; use mpsc when a task needs to send multiple messages over its lifetime.

prompt: I'm using tokio::sync::broadcast to fan out events to multiple subscribers. My second receiver misses early messages because it subscribed after the sender already sent them.
explanation: tokio::sync::broadcast delivers each message to all current subscribers from the point they subscribed. A receiver created after a send call will never see that message. Additionally, if a receiver falls behind by more than the channel capacity, recv() returns Err(RecvError::Lagged(n)) indicating how many messages were skipped — this is distinct from the receiver just not existing yet. If you need a subscriber to receive historical messages, consider a watch channel (which holds the latest value) or store messages in a Vec and replay them when a subscriber joins.

prompt: I want multiple tasks to watch a shared configuration value that can be updated at runtime. I tried using an mpsc channel but all listeners consume the same message. What's the right channel type?
explanation: The four tokio channel types serve different purposes: mpsc (each message to one receiver), oneshot (single message, consumed), broadcast (each message to all current receivers, may lag), and watch (latest value to all receivers, no lag, older values overwritten). For a shared configuration that many workers need to read and react to updates, watch is the right choice. Receivers call changed().await to wait for the next update, then borrow() to read the current value. watch::Receiver is Clone, so any number of workers can subscribe to the same Sender.

prompt: I have an unbounded mpsc channel and my producer is much faster than my consumer. Memory usage grows without bound. How do I add backpressure to a tokio channel?
explanation: mpsc::unbounded_channel creates a channel with no limit on queued items. The sender never blocks — it can enqueue faster than the consumer drains, leading to unbounded memory growth. mpsc::channel(n) creates a bounded channel with capacity n. When full, Sender::send().await yields the current task, giving the consumer time to drain the queue. This automatic backpressure propagates from consumer to producer: a slow consumer naturally slows the producer. Choose the buffer size based on how much latency is acceptable versus throughput; too small causes excessive context switching, too large wastes memory.
